core_story.problem: Introduction Diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) is becoming the cor- nerstone of data generation, where the forward process gradually contaminates the data with gaussian noises and the reverse denoising process is learned to generate new samples [12, 15].
core_story.limitation: However, due to intricate temporal and spatial correlations as well as high dimensionality of video, video diffusion generation remains a challenging task.
core_story.insight: Motivated by the success in image generation, some researchers are trying to extend the dif- fusion model from image to video by accompanying with *Corresponding author temporal-dimensional prediction/interpolation [9, 39, 49].
core_story.proposed_method: In this work, we propose an ex- plicit Spatio-Temporal Dual Diffusion (STDD) method by principledly extending the standard diffusion model to a spatio-temporal diffusion model for joint spatial and tem- poral noise propagation/reduction.
core_story.experimental_evidence: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our pro- posed STDD is more competitive over the state-of-the-art methods in the task of video generation/prediction as well as text-to-video generation.
core_story.summary: Introduction Diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) is becoming the cor- nerstone of data generation, where the forward process gradually contaminates the data with gaussian noises and the reverse denoising process is learned to generate new samples [12, 15]. However, due to intricate temporal and spatial correlations as well as high dimensionality of video, video diffusion generation remains a challenging task. Motivated by the success in image generation, some researchers are trying to extend the dif- fusion model from image to video by accompanying with *Corresponding author temporal-dimensional prediction/interpolation [9, 39, 49]. In this work, we propose an ex- plicit Spatio-Temporal Dual Diffusion (STDD) method by principledly extending the standard diffusion model to a spatio-temporal diffusion model for joint spatial and tem- poral noise propagation/reduction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our pro- posed STDD is more competitive over the state-of-the-art methods in the task of video generation/prediction as well as text-to-video generation.

1. Introduction
Diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) is becoming the cor-
nerstone of data generation, where the forward process
gradually contaminates the data with gaussian noises and
the reverse denoising process is learned to generate new
samples [12, 15]. Specifically, DPM has made significant
progress in producing high quality, realistic images [17,
23, 24, 27, 29, 33]. Motivated by the success in image
generation, some researchers are trying to extend the dif-
fusion model from image to video by accompanying with
*Corresponding author
temporal-dimensional prediction/interpolation [9, 39, 49].
However, due to intricate temporal and spatial correlations
as well as high dimensionality of video, video diffusion
generation remains a challenging task.
The existing video generation methods based on
DPMs [9, 13, 28] typically employ a standard diffusion pro-
cess by treating each frame of the video as an independent
diffusion process, which breaks the temporal correlation be-
tween frames. Concretely, in the noising/denoising process,
these video diffusion methods separately derive/sample
priors, and recover corresponding frames from different-
random priors. Due to different-random

image. To reach the inverse diffusion, we derive a proba-
bilistic reverse process, which guanrantees the feasibility of
the diffusion model. Inspired by the diffusion probabilis-
tic implicit diffusion (DDIM) model [33], further, we pro-
pose an accelerated spatio-temporal sampling method that
is both fast and computation-intensive. To facilitate learn-
ing, we decompose the model into spatial components rep-
resenting the standard diffusion model and temporal com-
ponents representing the frame-to-frame diffusion After a
series of derivation, we could reach a maneuverable solution
by using neural network. Overall, the spatio-temporal diffu-
sion enables the information of previous frames to be recur-
sively transferred to the current frame, which endows the
model good ability to capture temporal dependencies, and
can be robust to video consistency. Extensive experiments
demonstrate that our proposed STDD is more competitive
over the state-of-the-art methods in the task of video gen-
eration and prediction. In addition, we extend our STDD
to text-to-video generation to explore the video generation
capabilities.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Diffusion probabilistic model is becoming the corner-
stone of data generation, especially generating high-quality
images. As an extension, video diffusion generation is in
urgent need of a principled temporal-sequence diffusion
way, while the spatial-domain diffusion dominates most
video diffusion methods. In this work, we propose an ex-
plicit Spatio-Temporal Dual Diffusion (STDD) method by
principledly extending the standard diffusion model to a
spatio-temporal diffusion model for joint spatial and tem-
poral noise propagation/reduction. Mathematically, an
analysable dual diffusion process is derived to accumulate
information in temporal sequence as well as spatial domain.
Correspondingly, we theoretically derive a spatio-temporal
probabilistic reverse diffusion process and propose an ac-
celerated sampling way to reduce the inference cost. In
principle, the spatio-temporal dual diffusion enables the in-
formation of previous frames to be transferred to the cur-
rent frame, which thus could be beneficial for video con-
sistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our pro-
posed STDD is more competitive over the state-of-the-art
methods in the task of video generation/prediction as well
as text-to-video generation.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Diffusion probabilistic model (DPM) is becoming the cor-
nerstone of data generation, where the forward process
gradually contaminates the data with gaussian noises and
the reverse denoising process is learned to generate new
samples [12, 15]. Specifically, DPM has made significant
progress in producing high quality, realistic images [17,
23, 24, 27, 29, 33]. Motivated by the success in image
generation, some researchers are trying to extend the dif-
fusion model from image to video by accompanying with
*Corresponding author
temporal-dimensional prediction/interpolation [9, 39, 49].
However, due to intricate temporal and spatial correlations
as well as high dimensionality of video, video diffusion
generation remains a challenging task.
The existing video generation methods based on
DPMs [9, 13, 28] typically employ a standard diffusion pro-
cess by treating each frame of the video as an independent
diffusion process, which breaks the temporal correlation be-
tween frames. Concretely, in the noising/denoising process,
these video diffusion methods separately derive/sample
priors, and recover corresponding frames from different-
random priors. Due to different-random priors, the continu-
ity of generated video frames is still a bottleneck of video
diffusion generation, even though some historical (gener-
ated) frames could be used as a condition of the diffu-
sion for some-degree mitigation [25]. An endeavor to this
aspect is one recent work [20], decomposing video noise
into two components: video sharing base noise and frame-
independent residual noise, and then separately performing
the diffusion process with two different diffusion networks,
which greatly improves video consistency.
Despite some progress in video diffusion generation,
these existing methods could not well capture temporal de-
pendencies due to the use of independent priors for each
frame, which would often result into the nonsmoothness or
unconsistency of generated videos, especially confronting
with large appearance variations in temporal sequences. Es-
sentially, the current video diffusion methods lack a prin-
cipled diffusion process along time slices (w.r.t frame se-
quence), not just in the spatial domain (w.r.t every image).
In this work, we propose an explicit Spatio-Temporal
Dual Diffusion (STDD) method by principledly extend-
ing the standard diffusion model to a spatio-temporal dif-
fusion model for joint spatial and temporal frame propa-
gation. Mathematically, we derive an analysable dual dif-
fusion process by accumulating temporal frame informa-
tion. In particular, the historical frames are propagated
along with temporal sequence, while accompanying with

Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;

12575

the spatio-domain noises w.r.t diffusion on single-frame
image. To reach the inverse diffusion, we derive a proba-
bilistic reverse process, which guanrantees the feasibility of
the diffusion model. Inspired by the diffusion probabilis-
tic implicit diffusion (DDIM) model [33], further, we pro-
pose an accelerated spatio-temporal sampling method that
is both fast and computation-intensive. To facilitate learn-
ing, we decompose the model into spatial components rep-
resenting the standard diffusion model and temporal com-
ponents representing the frame-to-frame diffusion After a
series of derivation, we could reach a maneuverable solution
by using neural network. Overall, the spatio-temporal diffu-
sion enables the information of previous frames to be recur-
sively transferred to the current frame, which endows the
model good ability to capture temporal dependencies, and
can be robust to video consistency. Extensive experiments
demonstrate that our proposed STDD is more competitive
over the state-of-the-art methods in the task of video gen-
eration and prediction. In addition, we extend our STDD
to text-to-video generation to explore the video generation
capabilities.
In summary, our main contributions are three folds: i)
propose an explicit spatio-temporal dual diffusion model,
mathematically integrating temporal- and spatial-domain
diffusion processes; ii) theoretically derive probabilistic re-
verse process and propose an accelerated sampling method;
iii) achieve more competitive performance on video gener-
ation.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this paper, we propose an explicit spatio-temporal dual
diffusion model, mathematically integrating temporal- and
spatial-domain diffusion processes. Then, we theoretically
derive probabilistic reverse process and propose an acceler-
ated sampling method. Both qualitative and quantitative ex-
periments demonstrated that our model achieve more com-
petitive performance on video generation.
